title: Expressiveness and Approximation Properties of Graph Neural Networks

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## EXPRESSIVENESS AND APPROXIMATION PROPERTIES OF GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS

## **Floris Geerts**

Department of Computer Science, University of Antwerp, Belgium floris.geerts@uantwerpen.be

## **Juan L. Reutter**

School of Engineering, Pontificia Universidad Cat´olica de Chile, Chile & IMFD, Chile jreutter@ing.puc.cl

## ABSTRACT

Characterizing the separation power of graph neural networks (GNNs) provides an understanding of their limitations for graph learning tasks. Results regarding separation power are, however, usually geared at specific GNN architectures, and tools for understanding arbitrary GNN architectures are generally lacking. We provide an elegant way to easily obtain bounds on the separation power of GNNs in terms of the Weisfeiler-Leman (WL) tests, which have become the yardstick to measure the separation power of GNNs. The crux is to view GNNs as expressions in a procedural tensor language describing the computations in the layers of the GNNs. Then, by a simple analysis of the obtained expressions, in terms of the number of indexes and the nesting depth of summations, bounds on the separation power in terms of the WL-tests readily follow. We use tensor language to define Higher-Order Message-Passing Neural Networks (or _k_ -MPNNs), a natural extension of MPNNs. Furthermore, the tensor language point of view allows for the derivation of universality results for classes of GNNs in a natural way. Our approach provides a toolbox with which GNN architecture designers can analyze the separation power of their GNNs, without needing to know the intricacies of the WL-tests. We also provide insights in what is needed to boost the separation power of GNNs.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) (Merkwirth & Lengauer, 2005; Scarselli et al., 2009) cover many popular deep learning methods for graph learning tasks (see Hamilton (2020) for a recent overview). These methods typically compute vector embeddings of vertices or graphs by relying on the underlying adjacency information. Invariance (for graph embeddings) and equivariance (for vertex embeddings) of GNNs ensure that these methods are oblivious to the precise representation of the graphs.

**Separation power.** Our primary focus is on the separation power of GNN architectures, i.e., on their ability to separate vertices or graphs by means of the computed embeddings. It has become standard to characterize GNN architectures in terms of the separation power of graph algorithms such as color refinement (CR) and _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman tests ( _k_ -WL), as initiated in Xu et al. (2019) and Morris et al. (2019). Unfortunately, understanding the separation power of any given GNN architecture requires complex proofs, geared at the specifics of the architecture. We provide a _tensor language-based technique_ to analyze the separation power of general GNNs.

**Tensor languages.** Matrix query languages (Brijder et al., 2019; Geerts et al., 2021b) are defined to assess the expressive power of linear algebra. Balcilar et al. (2021a) observe that, by casting various GNNs into the MATLANG (Brijder et al., 2019) matrix query language, one can use existing separation results (Geerts, 2021) to obtain upper bounds on the separation power of GNNs in terms of 1-WL and 2-WL. In this paper, we considerably extend this approach by defining, and studying, a new general-purpose tensor language specifically designed for modeling GNNs. As in Balcilar et al. (2021a), our focus on tensor languages allows us to obtain new insights about GNN architectures.

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

First, since tensor languages can only define invariant and equivariant graph functions, any GNN that can be cast in our tensor language inherits these desired properties. More importantly, the separation power of our tensor language is as closely related to CR and _k_ -WL as GNNs are. Loosely speaking, _if tensor language expressions use k_ + 1 _indices, then their separation power is bounded by k-_ WL _. Furthermore, if the maximum nesting of summations in the expression is t, then t rounds of k-_ WL _are needed to obtain an upper bound on the separation power._ A similar connection is obtained for CR and a fragment of tensor language that we call “guarded” tensor language.

We thus reduce problem of assessing the separation power of _any_ specific GNN architecture to the problem of specifying it in our tensor language, analyzing the number of indices used and counting their summation depth. This is usually much easier than dealing with intricacies of CR and _k_ -WL, as casting GNNs in our tensor language is often as simple as writing down their layer-based definition. We believe that this provides a nice toolbox for GNN designers to assess the separation power of their architecture. We use this toolbox to recover known results about the separation power of specific GNN architectures such as GINs (Xu et al., 2019), GCNs (Kipf & Welling, 2017), Folklore GNNs (Maron et al., 2019b), _k_ -GNNs (Morris et al., 2019), and several others. We also derive new results: we answer an open problem posed by Maron et al. (2019a) by showing that the separation power of Invariant Graph Networks ( _k_ -IGNs), introduced by Maron et al. (2019b), is bounded by ( _k −_ 1)-WL. In addition, we revisit the analysis by Balcilar et al. (2021b) of ChebNet (Defferrard et al., 2016), and show that CayleyNet (Levie et al., 2019) is bounded by 2-WL.

When writing down GNNs in our tensor language, the less indices needed, the stronger the bounds in terms of _k_ -WL we obtain. After all, ( _k −_ 1)-WL is known to be strictly less separating than _k_ -WL (Otto, 2017). Thus, it is important to minimize the number of indices used in tensor language expressions. We connect this number to the notion of _treewidth_ : expressions of treewidth _k_ can be translated into expressions using _k_ + 1 indices. This corresponds to optimizing expressions, as done in many areas in machine learning, by reordering the summations (a.k.a. variable elimination).

**Approximation and universality.** We also consider the ability of GNNs to approximate general invariant or equivariant graph functions. Once more, instead of focusing on specific architectures, we use our tensor languages to obtain general approximation results, which naturally translate to universality results for GNNs. We show: ( _k_ + 1)-index tensor language expressions suffice to approximate any (invariant/equivariant) graph function whose separating power is bounded by _k_ -WL, and we can further refine this by comparing the number of rounds in _k_ -WL with the summation depth of the expressions. These results provide a finer picture than the one obtained by Azizian & Lelarge (2021). Furthermore, focusing on “guarded” tensor expressions yields a similar universality result for CR, a result that, to our knowledge, was not known before. We also provide the link between approximation results for tensor expressions and GNNs, enabling us to transfer our insights into universality properties of GNNs. As an example, we show that _k_ -IGNs can approximate any graph function that is less separating than ( _k−_ 1)-WL. This case was left open in Azizian & Lelarge (2021).

In summary, we draw new and interesting connections between tensor languages, GNN architectures and classic graph algorithms. We provide a general recipe to bound the separation power of GNNs, optimize them, and understand their approximation power. We show the usefulness of our method by recovering several recent results, as well as new results, some of them left open in previous work.

**Related work.** Separation power has been studied for specific classes of GNNs (Morris et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Maron et al., 2019b; Chen et al., 2019; Morris et al., 2020; Azizian & Lelarge, 2021). A first general result concerns the bounds in terms of CR and 1-WL of Message-Passing Neural Networks (Gilmer et al., 2017; Morris et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019). Balcilar et al. (2021a) use the MATLANG matrix query language to obtain upper bounds on the separation power of various GNNs. MATLANG can only be used to obtain bounds up to 2-WL and is limited to matrices. Our tensor language is more general and flexible and allows for reasoning over the number of indices, treewidth, and summation depth of expressions. These are all crucial for our main results. The tensor language introduced resembles sum-MATLANG (Geerts et al., 2021b), but with the added ability to represent tensors. Neither separation power nor guarded fragments were considered in Geerts et al. (2021b). See Section A in the supplementary material for more details. For universality, Azizian & Lelarge (2021) is closest in spirit. Our approach provides an elegant way to recover and extend their results. Azizian & Lelarge (2021) describe how their work (and hence also ours) encompasses previous works (Keriven & Peyr´e, 2019; Maron et al., 2019c; Chen et al., 2019). Our results use connections between _k_ -WL and logics (Immerman & Lander, 1990; Cai et al., 1992), and CR and

## 3 SPECIFYING GNNS

Many GNNs use linear algebra computations on vectors, matrices or tensors, interleaved with the application of activation functions or MLPs. To understand the separation power of GNNs, we introduce a specification language, TL, for tensor language, that allows us to specify any algebraic computation in a procedural way by explicitly stating how each entry is to be computed. We gauge the separation power of GNNs by specifying them as TL expressions, and syntactically analyzing the components of such TL expressions. This technique gives rise to _Higher-Order Message-Passing Neural Networks_ (or _k_ -MPNNs), a natural extension of MPNNs (Gilmer et al., 2017). For simplicity, we present TL using summation aggregation only but arbitrary aggregation functions on multisets of real values can be used as well (Section C.5 in the supplementary material).

## 4 SEPARATION POWER OF TENSOR LANGUAGES

Our first main results concern the characterization of the separation power of tensor languages in terms of the color refinement and _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman algorithms. We provide a finegrained characterization by taking the number of rounds of these algorithms into account. This will allow for measuring the separation power of classes of GNNs in terms of their number of layers.

## 4.2 MAIN RESULTS

We first provide a link between _k_ -WL and tensor language expressions using _k_ + 1 index variables: **Theorem 4.1.** _For each k ≥_ 1 _and any collection_ Ω _of functions, ρ_ 1�vwl _[(] k[∞][)]_ � = _ρ_ 1�TL _k_ +1(Ω)� _._

This theorem gives us new insights: if we wish to understand how a new GNN architecture compares against the _k_ -WL algorithms, all we need to do is to show that such an architecture can be represented in TL _k_ +1(Ω), i.e., is a _k_ -MPNN, an arguably much easier endeavor. As an example of how to use this result, it is well known that triangles can be detected by 2-WL but not by 1-WL. Thus, in order to design GNNs that can detect triangles, layer definitions in TL3 rather than TL2 should be used.
We can do much more, relating the rounds of _k_ -WL to the notion of summation depth of TL(Ω) expressions. We also present present similar results for functions computing graph embeddings.

We also consider “augmented” GNNs, which are combined with a preprocessing step in which higher-order graph information is computed. In the supplementary material (Section D.3) we show how TL encodes the preprocessing step, and how this leads to separation bounds in terms of _k_ -WL, where _k_ depends on the treewidth of the graph information used. ## 7 CONCLUSION

Connecting GNNs and tensor languages allows us to use our analysis of tensor languages to understand the separation and approximation power of GNNs. The number of indices and summation depth needed to represent the layers in GNNs determine their separation power in terms of color refinement and Weisfeiler-Leman tests. The framework of _k_ -MPNNs provides a handy toolbox to understand existing and new GNN architectures, and we demonstrate this by recovering several results about the power of GNNs presented recently in the literature, as well as proving new results.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a tensor language (TL) framework to characterize the separation and approximation power of various Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures by mapping them to the Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL) hierarchy. By analyzing the number of indices and summation depth in TL expressions, the authors provide a unified method to derive upper bounds on GNN expressivity.

The paper offers a mathematically elegant and powerful toolkit for analyzing GNN expressivity that simplifies complex proofs regarding WL-hierarchy equivalence. Its ability to unify diverse architectures—from GINs to IGNs—within a single formal language is a significant contribution to the theoretical understanding of graph learning models.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity in 'Guarded' TL and Color Refinement Equivalence: In Theorem 4.3, the authors claim an equivalence between guarded tensor language (GTL) and color refinement (CR). However, the definition of guardedness in Section 4.2 seems to conflate the local neighborhood aggregation of MPNNs with the specific label-refinement mechanism of CR. Standard CR is known to have subtleties concerning the distinction between adjacent and non-adjacent vertices, and the paper's definition of 'guarded' summation might be too restrictive to capture the full power of 1-WL as defined in the literature. The authors should clarify if their GTL is strictly equivalent to the version of CR that distinguishes non-neighbors, or if it maps to a weaker variant, as this distinction is central to the separation power claims.

- Lack of Explicit Treewidth-to-Complexity Mapping: The authors highlight the role of treewidth in optimizing TL expressions (Proposition 4.5) and suggest that this relates to computational feasibility. However, the paper stops short of providing a formal link between the treewidth of an expression and the actual runtime complexity of the corresponding GNN layer. While they mention variable elimination, a formal statement regarding the computational cost (e.g., in terms of n and k) for evaluating expressions of treewidth k would be necessary to support the claim that this framework aids in designing scalable architectures.

- Assumptions on Activation Functions in Universality Results: The approximation and universality results in Section 6 rely on the assumption that the set of functions $\Omega$ contains all continuous functions or MLPs. While this is standard for universal approximation theorems, the paper does not address how the choice of non-linear activation functions (e.g., ReLU vs. Sigmoid) interacts with the summation depth $t$ to limit the approximation capacity in practice. Since the paper aims to provide a 'toolbox' for designers, it should explicitly state whether the approximation bounds hold for any non-linearity or if specific properties (like those required for the Stone-Weierstrass theorem) are strictly necessary for the result to hold.

- Incomplete Treatment of Spectral GNNs: The discussion of CayleyNets in Section 5 and the supplementary material claims they are bounded by 2-WL, but this analysis seems to rely on a translation into TL that may not fully capture the spectral filtering mechanism. Spectral GNNs often involve polynomial filters that can, in theory, approximate arbitrary functions on the graph spectrum. The paper should clarify whether the TL representation of these filters is exhaustive or if there exist spectral architectures that fall outside the current TL-based expressivity bounds.

- Lack of a concrete worked example for treewidth-based optimization: The paper introduces a significant claim in Proposition 4.5 that expressions of treewidth k can be translated into expressions using k+1 indices via variable elimination. While the paper provides a brief mention of path-counting, it does not demonstrate this optimization process on a more complex, non-trivial architecture. A worked example showing the step-by-step translation of a specific, higher-order GNN layer (e.g., a 3-IGN layer or a specific subgraph-counting GNN) into a more efficient k+1 index form would clarify the practical utility of this result. This is essential to show that the 'toolbox' is usable by GNN designers, rather than just a theoretical abstraction.

- Absence of a concrete counterexample for the separation power of GNNs: The paper provides a theoretical framework for bounding the separation power of GNNs using k-WL, but it lacks a concrete example of a specific, commonly used GNN architecture that demonstrably fails to distinguish a pair of graphs that are not k-WL equivalent. Providing a specific pair of graphs (e.g., the standard Cai-Fürer-Immerman graphs for k=2) and showing how a specific, widely used GNN architecture (like a standard GCN) fails to distinguish them within the proposed Tensor Language framework would significantly strengthen the paper's claim that its toolbox provides actionable insights for developers.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the precise relationship between the guarded fragment of TL and the specific variant of color refinement used, addressing the distinction between neighbor and non-neighbor information.

- Provide a formal analysis linking the treewidth of TL expressions to the computational complexity of the corresponding GNN layers to substantiate claims about scalability.

- Explicitly state the requirements on the activation functions $\Omega$ for the universality results to hold, and discuss potential limitations when using standard non-linearities like ReLU.

- Expand the discussion on spectral GNNs to justify why the TL representation is sufficient to capture their full expressive potential.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the inclusion direction for WL hierarchy
comments.quote: The following strict inclusions are known: for all $k\geq 1$, $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{k+1}^{(t)})\subset\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{k}^{(t)})$ and $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{1}^{(t)})\subset\rho_{1}(\mathsf{cr}^{(t)})$
comments.explanation: The definition of the equivalence relation $\rho_1(\mathcal{F})$ implies that if $\mathcal{F}_A \subset \mathcal{F}_B$, then $\rho_1(\mathcal{F}_B) \subseteq \rho_1(\mathcal{F}_A)$, because a larger set of functions imposes more constraints, making the relation finer. Since $k$-WL is strictly more powerful than $(k+1)$-WL, the set of functions represented by $k$-WL is a superset of those represented by $(k+1)$-WL. Consequently, the equivalence relation induced by $k$-WL must be a subset of the relation induced by $(k+1)$-WL. Please reverse the inclusion to $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{k}^{(t)})\subset\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{k+1}^{(t)})$ to align with standard expressivity hierarchies.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of k-MPNNs
comments.quote: We use tensor language to define Higher-Order Message-Passing Neural Networks (or $k$-MPNNs), a natural extension of MPNNs.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces '$k$-MPNNs' as an extension of MPNNs, but the text lacks a formal definition or precise characterization of this class within the tensor language framework. Because the paper relies on this class to establish bounds relative to the $k$-WL hierarchy, the absence of a rigorous definition hinders verification. Please provide a formal definition specifying the constraints on tensor expressions—such as index count and summation depth—that define a $k$-MPNN.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the bound for graph versions of k-MPNNs
comments.quote: If vertex embedding methods are $k$-$\mathsf{MPNN}$s, then so are their graph versions, which are then bounded by $\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(\infty)}$ by our Theorem $4.4$.
comments.explanation: The claim that graph-level versions of $k$-MPNNs are bounded by $\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(\infty)}$ appears inconsistent with the logic of tuple-based embeddings. A global readout layer $\sum_{v \in V} \text{emb}(G, v)$ introduces an additional summation over the vertex set, which increases the required tuple size for the global aggregation step. This effectively shifts the representation to $\mathsf{TL}_{k+2}(\Omega)$, suggesting the graph version should be bounded by $\mathsf{gwl}_{k+1}^{(\infty)}$. Please clarify this index shift or adjust the bound to maintain consistency with the logic of tuple-based embeddings.

comments.title: Ambiguity in aggregation function scope
comments.quote: For simplicity, we present $\mathsf{TL}$ using summation aggregation only but arbitrary aggregation functions on multisets of real values can be used as well
comments.explanation: The paper claims arbitrary aggregation functions are supported, but the $\mathsf{TL}$ syntax relies on explicit index variables $x_2$ ranging over $[n]$. If an aggregation function like 'max' or 'mean' is used, the semantics of the summation $\sum_{x_2}$ must be replaced by a multiset aggregation operator $\text{AGG}_{x_2 \in N(x_1)}$. The current definition of $\psi_s(x_1)$ is tied to the summation operator. Please add a formal definition of the aggregation operator $\text{AGG}$ to the $\mathsf{TL}$ syntax to clarify how it interacts with index variables.

comments.title: Ambiguous index notation for output dimensions
comments.quote: We have seen that for each $s\in[\ell]$, $\llbracket\psi_{s},v\rrbracket_{G}=F_{vs}^{\prime}$ with $\psi_{s}\in\mathsf{TL}_{2}(\sigma)$.
comments.explanation: The variable $s$ is used here to iterate over the output dimension $\ell$, but it was previously defined as the number of free variables in the tuple $\bm{v} \in V_G^s$. This creates a collision where $s$ represents both the tuple size and the feature index. Please use a different index, such as $j \in [\ell]$, to distinguish the output dimension from the tuple size.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a tensor language framework to bound the separation and approximation power of GNNs using Weisfeiler-Leman tests and color refinement. The syntactic analysis of index count and summation depth offers a clean alternative to traditional logical characterizations. While the theoretical connections are conceptually sound, the manuscript relies heavily on supplementary material for core proofs and makes restrictive assumptions in its approximation results. Addressing these gaps and discussing practical architectural constraints is necessary for the work to meet top-venue standards.

The paper introduces a tensor language framework to analyze the separation and approximation power of GNNs in terms of WL tests and color refinement. The approach offers a clean syntactic method to bound expressiveness and recovers several known results. The theoretical connections between index count, summation depth, and WL rounds are conceptually appealing. However, the reliance on supplementary material for core proofs and the limited discussion of architectural and computational constraints weaken the overall impact.

Overview issues:

- Compactness assumption in approximation theorem ignores discrete graph topology: Theorem 6.1 and Corollary 6.2 establish universal approximation results by assuming the space of graphs $γ_s$ is compact, justified solely by restricting vertex labels to a compact set $K$. This overlooks the discrete nature of graph topologies. Since the set of non-isomorphic graphs of size $n$ is finite, the space is trivially compact, but the theorem effectively only guarantees approximation over label values, not over graph structures or varying sizes. This limits the practical relevance of the universality claims, as GNNs are typically deployed on unseen graph topologies. The authors should explicitly clarify that the approximation holds pointwise over label assignments for fixed graph structures, and discuss how this aligns with or diverges from graphon-based universality results.

- Heavy reliance on supplementary material for core proofs: Core theoretical contributions, including Proposition 4.5 on treewidth-based index reduction and Theorem 5.1 bounding $(k+1)$-IGNs, are deferred entirely to the supplementary material. For a theoretical paper aiming to establish a general framework, these proofs form the backbone of the claimed results. Readers cannot fully verify the separation power bounds or the translation from tensor language to WL hierarchies without flipping to the appendix. The main text should include at least high-level proofs or detailed lemmas for these propositions to ensure self-containment and allow reviewers to assess the technical rigor directly.

- Syntactic summation depth does not capture skip connections or parallel branches: Theorem 4.2 maps the syntactic summation depth of TL expressions directly to the number of rounds in $k$-WL. This correspondence assumes a strict one-to-one relationship between nesting depth and network depth. However, modern GNN architectures frequently employ skip connections, residual blocks, or parallel aggregation branches that do not increase syntactic depth but significantly alter information flow. The current framework may underestimate the expressiveness of such architectures. The authors should extend the TL syntax to account for parallel compositions or residual connections, or explicitly state how depth $t$ should be interpreted for non-sequential architectures.

- Treewidth-based index reduction lacks rigorous connection to WL separation in the main text: Proposition 4.5 claims that TL expressions of treewidth $k$ are equivalent to those in $αTL_{k+1}(Ω)$, implying a reduction in WL separation bounds. While treewidth is a standard measure in tensor networks, the direct link between expression treewidth and the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy requires careful justification. The paper states that reordering summations reduces indices, but it does not explain how this algebraic optimization preserves or alters the logical equivalence classes defined by WL. A clearer exposition of how treewidth decomposition maps to WL indistinguishability would strengthen the theoretical foundation.

- Computational intractability of high-order bounds is underdiscussed: The framework successfully bounds higher-order GNNs by $k$-WL, but it glosses over the exponential computational cost $O(n^k)$ associated with these tests. The introduction notes this limitation in the literature review, but the main results do not discuss how the proposed framework addresses or mitigates it. Claiming that a GNN is bounded by $k$-WL is theoretically informative but practically limited if $k ≥ 2$. The authors should add a discussion on the trade-offs between expressiveness and tractability, and perhaps explore how the TL framework can guide the design of GNNs that approximate $k$-WL power with polynomial complexity.

- Worked example of TL analysis on a modern GNN architecture: The paper positions the tensor language as a practical toolbox for GNN designers, yet it only applies the framework to established architectures like GINs and GCNs. This leaves the central claim unverified for the complex models that actually drive current research. Readers will wonder whether the index-counting method scales to attention-based or subgraph-based designs without becoming unwieldy. The analysis should include a fully worked case study of a non-trivial architecture, such as a Graph Transformer or a hypergraph convolution. Explicitly mapping its layers to TL expressions, counting indices, and deriving the resulting WL bound would demonstrate the framework's utility beyond recovering known results.

- Treewidth optimization lacks demonstration on realistic GNN layer definitions: Proposition 4.5 claims that minimizing expression treewidth through variable elimination tightens Weisfeiler-Leman bounds, but the paper only illustrates this with a toy path-counting formula. A structural gap exists because the framework never applies this optimization to an actual GNN layer. Without a concrete computation, it is impossible to gauge how much index reduction translates to tighter separation bounds in practice. The authors should select a standard higher-order layer, such as a 3-hop message passing module or a clique-aggregation block, and compute its treewidth before and after reordering summations. Showing the resulting drop in index count would validate the theoretical connection between algebraic optimization and expressiveness.

- Attention mechanisms and continuous feature dependence are not modeled: The tensor language treats activation functions and MLPs as opaque operators that do not influence separation power, effectively ignoring how attention weights dynamically reweight indices based on continuous node features. This abstraction creates a disconnect between the formal framework and modern GNNs that rely on feature-dependent routing to break WL symmetries. The paper does not explain how to incorporate attention scores into the index structure or whether the black-box assumption holds when weights depend on the input graph. To make the framework applicable to contemporary architectures, the authors should either extend the syntax to track feature-dependent weights or provide a rigorous justification for why continuous reweighting does not alter the WL separation class.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents a valuable theoretical framework, but the deferred proofs for key propositions and the narrow compactness assumption in the approximation theorem require substantial clarification. Addressing the gap between syntactic depth and modern GNN architectures, as well as discussing computational trade-offs, is necessary for publication.

Revision targets:

- Move core proofs for Proposition 4.5 and Theorem 5.1 into the main text or provide detailed lemmas to ensure self-containment.

- Clarify that the approximation results in Theorem 6.1 apply pointwise to label assignments on fixed graph structures, and explicitly contrast this with graphon-based universality.

- Extend the TL framework to account for skip connections, residual blocks, or parallel branches, and explain how summation depth maps to these architectures.

- Add a dedicated discussion on the computational intractability of high-$k$ WL bounds and how the framework guides practical GNN design.

comments.title: Equivariance definition incorrectly states invariance
comments.quote: More generally, $f:\mathcal{G}_{s}\to\mathbb{F}$ is *equivariant* if $f(\sigma\star G,\sigma\star\bm{v})=f(G,\bm{v})$ for any permutation $\sigma$.
comments.explanation: The equation provided defines invariance under the diagonal group action, not equivariance. Equivariance requires the output to transform according to a representation of the group. Without specifying the action on $\mathbb{F}$, the property described is invariance. This mislabeling will confuse readers comparing your results to standard GNN literature where these concepts are distinct. Please either specify the group action on the codomain that makes the function equivariant, or rename the property to "invariant under joint permutation" to align with standard terminology.

comments.title: Mismatched parentheses and missing aggregation in k-WL update
comments.quote: For $t\geq 0$, $k$-WL revises the label according to $\mathsf{wl}_{k}^{i t+1)}(G,\bm{v}):=(\mathsf{wl}_{k}^{i t)}(G,\bm{v}),M)$ with $M:=\left\{\left\{\left\{\mathsf{atp}_{k+1}(G,\bm{v}u),\mathsf{wl}_{k}^{i t)}(G,\bm{v}[u/1]),\ldots,\mathsf{wl}_{k}^{i t)}(G,\bm{v}[u/k])\right)\mid u\in V_{G}\right\}\right\}$
comments.explanation: The update rule contains mismatched parentheses and uses `\right)` instead of `\right\}` for the multiset delimiter. These syntactic errors obscure the mathematical definition. Additionally, no explicit aggregation or hashing function is specified between the current label and the neighbor multiset, which is standard in GNN literature to ensure the update is well-defined. Rewrite the update rule to explicitly state the aggregation function and correct the bracket matching to ensure the equation is mathematically well-formed.

comments.title: 1-WL and Color Refinement are equivalent, not strictly nested
comments.quote: The following strict inclusions are known: for all $k\geq 1$, $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{k+1}^{(\mathsf{t})})\subset\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{k}^{(\mathsf{t})})$ and $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{1}^{(\mathsf{t})})\subset\rho_{1}(\mathsf{cr}^{(\mathsf{t})})$ *(Otto, 2017; Grohe, 2021)*.
comments.explanation: The 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman algorithm (1-WL) and Color Refinement (CR) execute the exact same vertex coloring updates at every round $t$, meaning their induced equivalence relations on vertex pairs are identical. Consequently, the separation power equivalence relations satisfy $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{1}^{(\mathsf{t})}) = \rho_{1}(\mathsf{cr}^{(\mathsf{t})})$, not a strict inclusion. Replace the strict inclusion symbol with an equality sign to accurately reflect the known hierarchy results.

comments.title: Ill-defined supremum domain and false compactness claim
comments.quote: We assume that $\mathcal{G}_{s}$ is a compact space by requiring that vertex labels come from a compact set $K\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{\ell_{0}}$.
comments.explanation: Restricting vertex labels to a compact set $K$ does not make the space of graphs $\mathcal{G}_{s}$ compact. The space of all graphs (of arbitrary size and topology) with labels in $K$ is not compact under any standard topology, and the supremum over all $G$ and $\bm{v}$ is generally unbounded or ill-defined. Uniform convergence over an unbounded or discrete combinatorial space requires careful topological framing. Specify the exact domain over which the supremum is evaluated (e.g., restrict to graphs of a fixed size $n$, or define a specific compact topology on the space of graphons) to address the ill-defined uniform topology.

comments.title: Mismatch between discrete graph structures and continuous approximation assumptions
comments.quote: Our next result is based on a generalized Stone-Weierstrass Theorem *(Timofte, 2005)*, also used in *Azizian and Lelarge (2021)*.
comments.explanation: The proof invokes a continuous Stone-Weierstrass theorem, which applies to algebras of continuous functions on compact topological spaces. However, $\mathcal{G}_{s}$ consists of discrete combinatorial objects (graphs with labeled vertices). Even if restricted to fixed size $n$, the domain is discrete, making uniform convergence equivalent to pointwise convergence and rendering topological separation conditions trivial or inapplicable. Clarify whether the approximation results apply to a continuous relaxation of graphs (like graphons) or if the Stone-Weierstrass reference is being applied to the discrete feature space $\mathbb{R}^{|V(G)| \times \ell_0}$ for a fixed graph size $n$.

comments.title: Missing point-separation condition in Theorem 6.1
comments.quote: For any $\ell$, and any set $\mathcal{F}$ of functions, concatenation and function closed for $\ell$, we have: $\overline{\mathcal{F}_{\ell}}=\{f:\mathcal{G}_{s}\to\mathbb{R}^{\ell}\mid\rho_{s}(\mathcal{F})\subseteq\rho_{s}(f)\}$.
comments.explanation: The Stone-Weierstrass framework requires the function class to separate points on the domain (or quotient domain) to guarantee density. Without this condition, $\mathcal{F}$ could consist solely of constant functions, which trivially satisfy concatenation- and function-closure, yet its uniform closure remains constants. The right-hand side would then include all functions constant on equivalence classes, making the equality false. The theorem statement must explicitly require that $\mathcal{F}$ separates points on $\mathcal{G}_s / \sim_{\mathcal{F}}$.

comments.title: Incorrect separation power bound for standard MPNNs
comments.quote: Then, since most classes of GNNs are concatenation-closed and allow the application of arbitrary MLPs, this implies that your GNNs can only approximate functions $f$ that are no more separating than $\mathsf{cr}^{(t)}$ (respectively, $\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)}$, $\mathsf{vwl}_{k}^{(t)}$ or $\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(\infty)}$).
comments.explanation: The text asserts that standard MPNNs are bounded by Color Refinement ($\mathsf{cr}^{(t)}$). This is inaccurate because Color Refinement is strictly weaker than 1-WL, whereas established results show that standard MPNNs are equivalent to 1-WL in separation power. This claim contradicts both the broader literature and the paper's own Theorem 4.2, which maps $\mathsf{TL}_2$ (the class containing standard MPNNs) to $1$-$\mathsf{WL}$. The bound for vertex-embedding MPNNs should be revised to $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{1}^{(t)})\subseteq\rho_{1}(f)$. If the authors intend to refer to graph-level readouts, the appropriate bound is $\rho_{0}(\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)})\subseteq\rho_{0}(f)$, but the vertex-level statement as written is wrong.

comments.title: Unjustified weakening to infinite WL rounds
comments.quote: If vertex embedding methods are $k$-$\mathsf{MPNN}$s, then so are their graph versions, which are then bounded by $\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(\infty)}$ by our Theorem $4.4$.
comments.explanation: The authors abruptly switch from a finite-round bound to an infinite-round bound $\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(\infty)}$ for the general $k$-$\mathsf{MPNN}$ case. If the vertex embeddings are computed in $t$ steps, the graph version (with an additional readout step) operates at depth $t+1$. Theorem 4.4 should yield a bound of $\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(t+1)}$, not $\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(\infty)}$. Claiming the infinite bound is technically true but trivially weak, and it obscures the precise expressiveness characterization. Replace $\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(\infty)}$ with $\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(t+1)}$ to maintain consistency with the finite-layer derivation, or provide a rigorous justification for why the graph version inherently requires the full $k$-WL hierarchy.

comments.title: Misattribution of MATLAB matrix query language to Balcilar et al.
comments.quote: *Balcilar et al. (2021a)* use the MATLAB matrix query language to obtain upper bounds on the separation power of various GNNs. MATLAB can only be used to obtain bounds up to $2$-WL and is limited to matrices.
comments.explanation: The attribution here is inaccurate. Balcilar et al. (2021a) relies on spectral graph theory and eigenvalue analysis to bound GNN expressiveness, rather than the MATLAB matrix query language. The MATLAB matrix query language, specifically sum-MATLANG, was introduced by Geerts et al. (2021b), which the authors cite immediately afterward. Furthermore, Geerts et al. (2021b) explicitly analyzes separation power, demonstrating that sum-MATLANG corresponds to the 1-Weisfeiler-Lehman test and discussing extensions toward 2-WL. Claiming that separation power was not considered in Geerts et al. (2021b) is therefore misleading. The authors should disentangle these two distinct lines of work, correctly attribute the matrix query language to Geerts et al. (2021b), and accurately summarize what each prior work actually contributes to the tensor language framework.

comments.title: Inconsistent direction of separation power claim
comments.quote: As an example, we show that $k$-IGNs can approximate any graph function that is less separating than $(k-1)$-WL.
comments.explanation: The summary claims that $k$-IGNs approximate functions 'less separating' than $(k-1)$-WL. However, the corresponding formal result (Theorem 5.1, per the cross-references) states that the closure of $(k+1)$-IGNs consists of functions $f$ satisfying $\rho_1(\mathsf{vwl}_k^{(\infty)}) \subseteq \rho_1(f)$. This set inclusion indicates that the approximable functions are at least as separating as the algorithm, not less separating. The informal summary reverses the logical direction of the separation power bound. Rewrite 'less separating than' to 'at least as separating as' to match the theorem's formal statement and standard definitions in this literature.

comments.title: Conclusion claims approximation power not covered in results
comments.quote: Connecting GNNs and tensor languages allows us to use our analysis of tensor languages to understand the separation and approximation power of GNNs. The number of indices and summation depth needed to represent the layers in GNNs determine their separation power in terms of color refinement and Weisfeiler-Leman tests.
comments.explanation: The formal results section exclusively introduces $\mathsf{TL}$ and $k$-MPNNs to analyze the separation power of GNNs through syntactic analysis of index variables and summation. It contains no definitions, theorems, or discussions regarding the approximation power of GNNs. The conclusion asserts that the framework enables understanding of both separation and approximation power, but the formal results do not establish the latter. Remove "and approximation" from the first sentence to accurately reflect the scope of the formal results, or add a formal subsection addressing approximation guarantees.

comments.title: Undefined distinction between 1-WL and Color Refinement
comments.quote: Our version of $1$-WL differs from CR in that $1$-WL also uses information from non-adjacent vertices; this distinction only matters for vertex embeddings *(Grohe, 2021)*.
comments.explanation: The claim that 1-WL uses information from non-adjacent vertices is stated without formal definition. Standard 1-WL (color refinement) aggregates only over immediate neighbors. The distinction likely refers to 1-WL aggregating over the global multiset of all vertex colors at each step, rather than just neighbors. To avoid ambiguity and ensure readers can verify the claim, explicitly define the 1-WL update rule and clarify how it incorporates global information compared to the local neighbor aggregation in CR. Add the explicit update formula for 1-WL to resolve the ambiguity.

comments.title: Missing representability bridge for $k$-MPNN claim
comments.quote: An immediate consequence is that when a GNN is a $k$-MPNN, it is automatically invariant or equivariant, depending on whether graph or vertex tuple embeddings are considered.
comments.explanation: The claim about $k$-MPNNs does not follow immediately from Proposition 3.1 alone; it requires the intermediate result that $k$-MPNNs can be expressed within the tensor language framework. Without explicitly invoking this representability result, the logical connection is broken. The authors should either move this consequence to follow the representability theorem or explicitly state the bridge in this paragraph.

comments.title: Incorrect count of multiplications in both examples
comments.quote: These examples show that it is not the number of multiplications (in both cases two) that gives power, it is how variables are connected to each other.
comments.explanation: Both examples actually contain three edge factors, not two. The path expression $\sum_{x_{2}}\sum_{x_{3}}\sum_{x_{4}}E(x_{1},x_{2})\cdot E(x_{2},x_{3})\cdot E(x_{3},x_{4})$ clearly has three $E$ terms. The clique expression $\sum_{x_{2}}\sum_{x_{3}}E(x_{1},x_{2})\cdot E(x_{1},x_{3})\cdot E(x_{2},x_{3})$ also contains three $E$ terms. Claiming both cases involve two multiplications is factually wrong and undermines the intended contrast between variable connectivity and operation count. Change "two" to "three".

comments.title: Misattribution of WL bound theorem and incorrect depth superscript
comments.quote: h one. Proposition $4.5$ tells that the layer is bounded by $\mathsf{wl}_{1}^{(3)}$ (and in fact by $\mathsf{cr}^{(3)}$) in separation power. If
comments.explanation: The separation power bound follows from Theorem 4.2, which establishes the equivalence between $\mathsf{TL}_{k+1}$ and $k$-$\mathsf{WL}$, not Proposition 4.5 (which addresses treewidth reduction). Additionally, the superscript $(3)$ denotes the number of aggregation rounds, but the layer described is a single-step operation, so the depth should be $(1)$. The exponent $3$ on $\bm{A}$ determines the path length, not the number of WL rounds. Rewrite to cite Theorem 4.2 and correct the depth superscript to $(1)$.

comments.title: Ambiguity in bound variable restriction for $\mathsf{TL}_k$
comments.quote:
le.

An important class of expressions are those that only use index variables $\{x_{1},\ldots,x_{k}\}$. We denote by $\mathsf{TL}_{k}(\Omega)$ the $k$-index variable fragment of $\mathsf{TL}(\Omega)$. T
comments.explanation: The definition states that expressions in $\mathsf{TL}_k(\Omega)$ 'only use index variables ${ x_1,…,x_k }$', but it does not explicitly clarify whether this restriction applies to bound variables introduced by the summation operator $\sum_x$. In standard $k$-variable fragments, both free and bound variables must be drawn from the allowed set. Without this clarification, an expression like $\sum_z E(x,z)$ with $z \notin { x_1,…,x_k }$ could ambiguously fall outside or inside the fragment. Add a clarifying phrase such as 'where both free and bound variables are chosen from ${ x_1,…,x_k }$' after the definition to align with standard logical fragment conventions.

comments.title: Variable shadowing obscures index count in treewidth example
comments.quote: deed, it is equivalent to the expression $\hat{\theta}(x_{1})=\sum_{x_{2}}E(x_{1},x_{2})\cdot\left(\sum_{x_{1}}E(x_{2},x_{1})\right)$ in $\mathsf{TL}_{2}^{(2)}$ (and in fact in $\mathsf{GTL}^{(2)}$).
comments.explanation: The inner summation reuses $x_1$ as a dummy variable, shadowing the free variable $x_1$ of the outer expression. This notation is mathematically imprecise and obscures the actual index count required for the reduction. The authors appear to intentionally reuse $x_1$ to keep the expression within $\mathsf{TL}_{2}$, but standard scoping rules would require a fresh dummy variable (e.g., $x_3$), which would temporarily place the expression in $\mathsf{TL}_{3}$. Add a clarifying remark explaining that variable reuse is permitted in this context to maintain the $\mathsf{TL}_{2}$ bound, or rewrite the expression using a fresh variable and explicitly note the temporary increase in index count before reduction.

comments.title: Overstatement on avoiding WL-test knowledge
comments.quote: Our approach provides a toolbox with which GNN architecture designers can analyze the separation power of their GNNs, without needing to know the intricacies of the WL-tests.
comments.explanation: The claim that designers can analyze separation power "without needing to know the intricacies of the WL-tests" is slightly overstated. The framework's core contribution relies on establishing a direct mapping between syntactic properties of TL expressions (index count, summation depth) and specific levels of the WL hierarchy. To interpret the resulting bounds, understand what "boosting separation power" entails, or compare architectures against established baselines, one must still understand the WL hierarchy's structure and limitations. The framework abstracts the mechanical execution of WL tests, but the hierarchy itself remains the necessary reference point. Rewrite the sentence to clarify that the approach abstracts the mechanics of WL tests, while retaining the WL hierarchy as the interpretive standard.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a tensor language (TL) framework for analyzing the expressiveness and approximation properties of graph neural networks (GNNs). It provides a systematic way to bound the separation power of GNNs in terms of the Weisfeiler-Lehman (WL) hierarchy and color refinement, based on the number of indices and summation depth in TL expressions. The framework is used to recover and extend known results, and to derive new bounds for architectures like IGNs and CayleyNets.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by providing a unified, syntactic framework for analyzing GNN expressiveness, which simplifies the derivation of separation bounds and extends to approximation results. The theoretical development is rigorous, and the connections to treewidth and variable elimination are insightful. The work is significant for the GNN theory community and meets the standards of a top venue.

Overview issues:

- Mapping between TL and practical GNN architectures is not fully precise: The paper claims that any GNN can be cast into TL expressions, but the mapping is only demonstrated for simple architectures like GINs, GCNs, and eGINs. Many modern GNNs use residual connections, normalization layers, attention mechanisms, or more complex aggregation functions (e.g., softmax in GAT). The paper does not show how these features are represented in TL, nor does it discuss whether such representations preserve the same bounds. For example, attention weights depend on features and are not simple linear operations, so representing them in TL may require additional indices or break the guardedness condition. The authors should either provide explicit constructions for a broader class of architectures or clearly state the limitations of TL in covering all GNN variants. Without this, the claim that TL provides a 'toolbox for GNN designers' is overstated.

- Equivalence between separation power and WL may break for continuous features: Theorems 4.1 and 4.2 establish equivalence between TL expressions and WL separation power, but this relies on the assumption that vertex features are from a compact set and that the functions in Ω are continuous. However, the WL test is typically defined for discrete labels, and its extension to continuous features is non-trivial. The paper does not address how the WL algorithm handles continuous initial colors, nor does it discuss whether the equivalence holds for arbitrary continuous feature spaces. This is a critical gap because many GNNs operate on continuous features, and the separation power of WL on such graphs may differ from the discrete case. The authors should clarify the assumptions on the feature space and provide a justification or reference for the continuous WL equivalence.

- Approximation results assume concatenation and function closure that may not hold for GNNs: Theorem 6.1 and Corollary 6.2 rely on the set of functions F being concatenation-closed and function-closed (for continuous functions). While the paper notes that these properties hold if Ω contains all continuous functions or all MLPs, it does not verify that typical GNN architectures (e.g., with fixed activation functions like ReLU) satisfy these properties. In practice, GNNs use specific activation functions and MLPs with bounded width and depth, which may not be closed under arbitrary continuous functions. The approximation results therefore assume an idealized setting that may not correspond to any implementable GNN. The authors should discuss the gap between the theoretical closure assumptions and practical GNNs, and clarify whether the results hold for finite-width, finite-depth networks.

- Treewidth-based optimization may not be feasible for complex GNN layers: Proposition 4.5 states that expressions of treewidth k can be reduced to use k+1 indices, which improves the separation bound. However, the paper does not provide an algorithm or complexity analysis for computing the treewidth of a TL expression derived from a GNN layer. For complex layers with many operations and index variables, computing treewidth may be computationally expensive (NP-hard in general). Moreover, the reduction may change the structure of the computation, potentially affecting numerical stability or the ability to apply activation functions. The paper should discuss the practical feasibility of treewidth reduction for GNN analysis, and whether it can be done automatically for arbitrary architectures.

- Missing discussion of limitations and edge cases: The paper does not include a dedicated limitations section or discuss scenarios where the TL framework may fail. For example, GNNs with random features, stochastic aggregation, or non-differentiable operations may not be representable in TL. Additionally, the framework assumes that all computations are over real numbers with exact arithmetic, ignoring numerical precision issues. The paper also does not address GNNs that use edge features or heterogeneous graphs, which are common in practice. A thorough discussion of these limitations would help readers understand the scope of the framework and avoid overgeneralization. The authors should add a section on limitations, covering at least the cases of non-standard architectures, discrete vs. continuous features, and numerical considerations.

- The connection to approximation results is not fully developed for graph-level functions: Theorem 4.4 provides separation bounds for graph-level functions, but the approximation results in Section 6 focus primarily on vertex-level functions. The paper mentions that graph-level approximation follows from readout layers, but does not provide explicit approximation theorems for graph-level functions analogous to Corollary 6.2. For example, it is not shown that k-MPNNs with readout can approximate any graph function that is less separating than gwl_k^{(∞)}. This gap weakens the claim of providing a 'complete toolbox' for GNN analysis. The authors should extend the approximation results to graph-level functions, or clearly state the limitations.

- No worked example showing separation bound is tight: The paper claims that TL expressions using k+1 indices have separation power bounded by k-WL, but it never demonstrates that this bound is tight — i.e., that there exists a concrete GNN architecture whose TL expression uses exactly k+1 indices and achieves the full separation power of k-WL. Without such an example, a reader cannot tell whether the bound is an overestimate or a precise characterization. The authors should construct a specific k-MPNN (for k=2, say) and exhibit a pair of non-isomorphic graphs that 2-WL separates but 1-WL does not, then verify that the k-MPNN indeed separates them. This would validate the central claim that the number of indices is the right measure of expressiveness.

- No concrete parametric model for treewidth reduction: Proposition 4.5 links treewidth to the number of indices, but the paper never computes the treewidth of a TL expression for a realistic GNN layer and shows that the reduction to fewer indices actually changes the separation bound. The example given (path counting) is trivial and does not involve a GNN architecture. The authors should pick a nontrivial GNN layer — for instance, one that computes triangle counts via a clique-based adjacency matrix — write its TL expression, compute its treewidth, apply the reduction, and then compare the k-WL bound before and after reduction. This would demonstrate the practical utility of the treewidth concept for GNN designers.

- No simulation or explicit construction verifying approximation claims: The approximation results (Section 6) are purely existential: they state that certain function classes can approximate all functions with no more separation power than a given WL level, but they provide no concrete construction or simulation to show that a specific GNN architecture (e.g., a 2-layer eGIN) can actually approximate a specific target function (e.g., counting triangles) to within a given error. In a theoretical paper, one would expect at least a constructive proof that the GNN can simulate the WL coloring process, and ideally a numerical illustration on small graphs. The authors should either provide an explicit construction (e.g., showing how to build MLP weights that approximate the WL update rule) or include a small-scale simulation that demonstrates the approximation claim for a simple target function.

- Missing guidance on estimating separation power in practice: The paper presents TL as a 'toolbox' for GNN designers, but it never explains how a practitioner would actually compute the number of indices or the summation depth for a given GNN architecture that is not already written in TL. For a complex architecture with attention, residual connections, or normalization, the translation to TL is nontrivial and may not be unique. The authors should provide a step-by-step procedure or algorithm for converting a GNN layer definition into a TL expression, and discuss the choices involved (e.g., how to handle softmax or layer normalization). Without such guidance, the claimed practical utility of the framework is unsubstantiated.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a novel and theoretically sound framework, but several key assumptions and practical limitations are not adequately addressed. The mapping between TL and real GNN architectures needs to be clarified, and the approximation results require a discussion of their idealization. The paper would benefit from a dedicated limitations section and a more thorough treatment of edge cases.

Revision targets:

- Add a section or subsection discussing the limitations of the TL framework, including: (a) which GNN architectures can and cannot be represented, (b) assumptions about continuous features and their impact on WL equivalence, (c) the gap between theoretical closure properties and practical GNNs, and (d) the feasibility of treewidth reduction.

- Provide explicit constructions or references for representing attention mechanisms, residual connections, and normalization layers in TL, or clearly state that these are not covered and why.

- Clarify the assumptions on the feature space for the WL equivalence theorems (Theorems 4.1-4.4) and justify the extension to continuous features, possibly by citing relevant literature.

- Extend the approximation results to graph-level functions (ρ_0) in a theorem analogous to Corollary 6.2, or explicitly state the limitations that prevent such an extension.

- Discuss the computational complexity of computing treewidth for TL expressions derived from GNN layers, and whether the reduction can be automated for practical analysis.

comments.title: Theorem 4.1 uses undefined notation
comments.quote:
4.1.

For each $k\geq 1$ and any collection $\Omega$ of functions, $\rho_{1}\big{(}\mathsf{vwl}_{k}^{(\mathsf{w}_{i})}\big{)}=\rho_{1}\big{(}\mathsf{TL}_{k+1}(\Omega)\big{)}$.
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathsf{vwl}_{k}^{(\mathsf{w}_{i})}$ is not defined. The paper defines $\mathsf{vwl}_k^{(t)}$ for a number of rounds $t$, but $\mathsf{w}_i$ appears to be a typo. This makes the theorem statement ambiguous. The intended notation is likely $\mathsf{vwl}_k^{(\infty)}$, matching the unlimited-round version used in Theorem 4.4 and the surrounding discussion. Please correct this.

comments.title: Theorem 4.2 notation typo
comments.quote: $\rho_1\big(\mathsf{vw}\mathsf{l}_k^{(t)}\big) = \rho_1\big(\mathsf{TL}_{k + 1}^{(t)}(\Omega)\big)$
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathsf{vw}\mathsf{l}_k^{(t)}$ appears to be a typographical error for $\mathsf{vwl}_k^{(t)}$ (vertex Weisfeiler-Lehman with $k$ colors and $t$ rounds). The paper consistently uses $\mathsf{vwl}_k^{(t)}$ elsewhere. The stray backslash before 'l' suggests an OCR artifact, but as a formal statement this is ambiguous. Rewrite as $\rho_1\big(\mathsf{vwl}_k^{(t)}\big) = \rho_1\big(\mathsf{TL}_{k + 1}^{(t)}(\Omega)\big)$ for consistency.

comments.title: Theorem 4.3 uses undefined notation
comments.quote: $\rho_1\big(\mathsf{cr}^{(t)}\big) = \rho_1\big(\mathsf{GTL}^{(t)}(\Omega)\big)$
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathsf{cr}^{(t)}$ is used in Theorem 4.3 but is not defined in the paper. The paper defines $\mathsf{cr}$ (color refinement) in the abstract and introduction, but $\mathsf{cr}^{(t)}$ (presumably $t$ rounds of color refinement) is never formally introduced. The paper later uses $\mathsf{cr}^{(t)}$ in the text without definition. Add a definition of $\mathsf{cr}^{(t)}$ before Theorem 4.3, e.g., 'Let $\mathsf{cr}^{(t)}$ denote the equivalence relation on vertices after $t$ rounds of color refinement.'

comments.title: Theorem 4.4 uses undefined notation
comments.quote: $\rho_0\big(\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)}\big) = \rho_0\big(\mathsf{TL}_2^{(t + 1)}(\Omega)\big) = \rho_0\big(\mathsf{gw}\mathsf{l}_1^{(t)}\big)$  (2)  $\rho_0\big(\mathsf{gw}\mathsf{l}_k^{(\infty)}\big) = \rho_0\big(\mathsf{TL}_{k + 1}(\Omega)\big).$
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)}$ is used in Theorem 4.4 but is not defined in the paper. The paper defines $\mathsf{gcr}$ (graph color refinement) in the text preceding the theorem, but $\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)}$ (presumably $t$ rounds of graph color refinement) is never formally introduced. Similarly, $\mathsf{gw}\mathsf{l}_1^{(t)}$ and $\mathsf{gw}\mathsf{l}_k^{(\infty)}$ appear with a stray backslash, likely intended as $\mathsf{gwl}_1^{(t)}$ and $\mathsf{gwl}_k^{(\infty)}$. Add a definition of $\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)}$ before Theorem 4.4, and correct the notation for graph WL to $\mathsf{gwl}_1^{(t)}$ and $\mathsf{gwl}_k^{(\infty)}$.

comments.title: Proposition 4.5 treewidth definition missing
comments.quote: Expressions in $\mathsf{TL}(\Omega)$ of treewidth $k$ are equivalent to expressions in $\mathsf{TL}_{k+1}(\Omega)$.
comments.explanation: Proposition 4.5 claims that expressions of treewidth $k$ are equivalent to expressions using $k+1$ index variables, but the definition of treewidth for TL expressions is not provided in the main text. The paper states 'Treewidth is defined in the supplementary material (Section G)', but the main text should at least sketch the definition or provide a reference to a standard notion (e.g., treewidth of the Gaifman graph of the expression). Without this, the proposition is not self-contained and its meaning is unclear. Add a brief definition or a precise reference in the main text, e.g., 'The treewidth of a TL expression is defined as the treewidth of its Gaifman graph (see Section G for details).'

comments.title: Theorem 4.4(1) summation depth mismatch
comments.quote: $\rho_0\big(\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)}\big) = \rho_0\big(\mathsf{TL}_2^{(t + 1)}(\Omega)\big) = \rho_0\big(\mathsf{gw}\mathsf{l}_1^{(t)}\big)$
comments.explanation: The claim $\rho_0(\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)}) = \rho_0(\mathsf{TL}_2^{(t+1)}(\Omega))$ states that $t$ rounds of graph color refinement correspond to summation depth $t+1$ in $\mathsf{TL}_2$. The paper's justification says 'the increase in summation depth by one is incurred by the additional aggregation needed to collect all vertex labels computed by $\mathsf{gwl}_1^{(t)}$'. However, the paper does not provide a formal proof or even a sketch of why exactly one extra layer of summation is needed. This is a nontrivial claim that requires justification. Add a brief explanation or a reference to a proof in the supplementary material.

comments.title: Theorem 4.4(2) missing round parameter
comments.quote: $\rho_0\big(\mathsf{gw}\mathsf{l}_k^{(\infty)}\big) = \rho_0\big(\mathsf{TL}_{k + 1}(\Omega)\big).$
comments.explanation: Part (2) of Theorem 4.4 states $\rho_0(\mathsf{gwl}_k^{(\infty)}) = \rho_0(\mathsf{TL}_{k+1}(\Omega))$, but the left-hand side uses $\infty$ rounds while the right-hand side uses $\mathsf{TL}_{k+1}(\Omega)$ without a round parameter. The paper earlier defines $\mathsf{TL}_{k+1}^{(t)}(\Omega)$ for bounded summation depth, but $\mathsf{TL}_{k+1}(\Omega)$ presumably allows unbounded depth. The theorem should clarify whether this equality holds for unbounded rounds on both sides, or if a round parameter is needed. Add a note that $\mathsf{TL}_{k+1}(\Omega)$ denotes expressions with arbitrary summation depth, matching the unlimited rounds of $\mathsf{gwl}_k^{(\infty)}$.

comments.title: Theorem 5.1's round multiplier is not justified
comments.quote:
Theorem 5.1.

For any $k\geq 1$, the separation power of a $t$-layered $(k+1)$-$\mathsf{IGN}$s is bounded by the separation power of $tk$ rounds of $k$-$\mathsf{WL}$.
comments.explanation: The main text provides no proof or even a sketch of the multiplier $k$ in the bound $tk$. The paper defers to the supplementary material, but the main text should at least outline the reasoning. The claim that '$(k+1)$-IGNs aggregate information in one layer that becomes accessible to $k$-WL in $k$ rounds' is an intuitive statement, not a rigorous justification. The authors should add a brief explanation of why the multiplier $k$ appears, or at minimum state that the proof is in the supplement and provide a sketch of the argument.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about CayleyNets and 2-WL
comments.quote: Finally, our approach can also be used to show that the spectral $\mathsf{CayleyNets}$ *(Levie et al., 2019)* are bounded in separation power by $2$-$\mathsf{WL}$.
comments.explanation: The paper provides no derivation or sketch of how CayleyNets, which use spectral filters based on the graph Laplacian, can be expressed in TL with at most 3 indices (for a 2-WL bound). The paper does not explain how to represent spectral operations (e.g., multiplication by eigenvectors or spectral filtering) in TL, nor does it specify the number of indices or summation depth required. Without this, the claim is unsubstantiated. The authors should either provide a brief derivation showing that CayleyNets can be cast into TL with at most 3 indices, or remove the claim if it cannot be supported in the main text.

comments.title: Missing justification for eGIN as 1-MPNN
comments.quote: In a similar way as for GINs, we can represent $\mathsf{eGIN}$ layers in $\mathsf{TL}_{2}^{(t)}(\Omega)$. That is, each $\mathsf{eGIN}^{(t)}$ is an $1$-$\mathsf{MPNN}^{(t)}$.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that eGINs can be represented in $\mathsf{TL}_{2}^{(t)}(\Omega)$ and are therefore 1-MPNNs, but it does not provide the explicit construction. The text only says 'in a similar way as for GINs,' but eGINs use an unguarded summation $\sum_{u\in V_G} \boldsymbol{F}_{u:}^{(t-1)}$, which requires a different treatment in TL (using $\sum_{x_1}$ instead of $\sum_{x_2}E(x_1,x_2)$). The paper should at least sketch how this unguarded sum is expressed in TL and why it requires only 2 indices. Without this, the reader cannot verify that eGINs indeed fall under the 1-MPNN framework. Add a brief derivation showing the TL expression for the unguarded sum and confirming that the number of indices is 2.

comments.title: Closure definition missing codomain
comments.quote:
0}}$. Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a set of functions $f:\mathcal{G}_{s}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{\ell_{f}}$ and define its closure $\overline{\mathcal{F}}$ as all functions $h$ from $\mathcal{G}_{s}$

which there exists a sequence $f_{1},f_{2},\ldots\in\mathcal{F}$ such that $\lim_{i\to\infty}\mathsf{sup}_{G,\bm{v}}\|f_{i}(G,\bm{v})-h(G,\bm{v})\|=0$ for some norm $\|.\|$. We ass
comments.explanation: The definition of $\overline{\mathcal{F}}$ is incomplete: it says 'all functions $h$ from $\mathcal{G}_{s}$' but does not specify the codomain. The closure should be defined as a subset of functions from $\mathcal{G}_{s}$ to some fixed target space (e.g., $\mathbb{R}^{\ell}$), otherwise the approximation condition $\|f_i(G,\bm{v})-h(G,\bm{v})\|$ is not well-defined because $f_i$ and $h$ may have different output dimensions.
