Rewrite the definition to specify the codomain explicitly, e.g., 'define its closure $\overline{\mathcal{F}_\ell}$ as all functions $h:\mathcal{G}_s\to\mathbb{R}^\ell$ for which there exists a sequence...'

comments.title: Undefined notation ρ_s in Theorem 6.1
comments.quote: $\overline{\mathcal{F}_{\ell}}=\{f:\mathcal{G}_{s}\to\mathbb{R}^{\ell}\mid\rho_{s}(\mathcal{F})\subseteq\rho_{s}(f)\}$.
comments.explanation: The notation $\rho_s(\mathcal{F})$ is used in the conclusion but has not been defined in this section or earlier in the paper. The paper defines $\rho(\mathcal{F}_\ell)$ in Corollary 6.2 and uses $\rho_1$ and $\rho_0$ in Section 4, but $\rho_s$ with subscript $s$ is not introduced. The subscript $s$ likely refers to the number of free indices (0 for graph-level, 1 for vertex-level), but this is not stated. The theorem statement is incomplete without a definition of $\rho_s$. Add a definition of $\rho_s$ before Theorem 6.1, e.g., 'For $s\in\mathbb{N}$, let $\rho_s(f)$ denote the equivalence relation on $s$-tuples of vertices induced by function $f$.'

comments.title: Undefined notation ρ(ℱ_ℓ) and ρ(alg) in Corollary 6.2
comments.quote: Under the assumptions of Theorem 6.1 and if $\rho(\mathcal{F}_{\ell})=\rho(\mathsf{alg})$, then $\overline{\mathcal{F}_{\ell}}=\{f:\mathcal{G}_{s}\to\mathbb{R}^{\ell}\mid\rho(\mathsf{alg})\subseteq\rho(f)\}$.
comments.explanation: The notation $\rho(\mathcal{F}_\ell)$ and $\rho(\mathsf{alg})$ is used without definition. In Theorem 6.1, the notation was $\rho_s(\mathcal{F})$ with a subscript $s$, but here the subscript is dropped. It is unclear whether $\rho$ without subscript refers to the same relation as $\rho_s$ for a specific $s$, or to a different relation (e.g., the graph-level separation for $s=0$). The paper should define $\rho(\mathcal{F}_\ell)$ explicitly, e.g., as the intersection of $\rho_s(f)$ over all $f\in\mathcal{F}_\ell$ and all relevant $s$, or clarify that it is shorthand for $\rho_s$ with the appropriate $s$ determined by context.

comments.title: Theorem 6.1 requires ℱ to be function-closed for ℓ
comments.quote: For any $\ell$, and any set $\mathcal{F}$ of functions, concatenation and function closed for $\ell$, we have: $\overline{\mathcal{F}_{\ell}}=\{f:\mathcal{G}_{s}\to\mathbb{R}^{\ell}\mid\rho_{s}(\mathcal{F})\subseteq\rho_{s}(f)\}$.
comments.explanation: The theorem statement says 'concatenation and function closed for $\ell$', but the earlier definition of function-closed requires fixing $\ell$: 'for a fixed $\ell\in\mathbb{N}$: for any $f\in\mathcal{F}$ such that $f:\mathcal{G}_{s}\to\mathbb{R}^{p}$, also $g\circ f:\mathcal{G}_{s}\to\mathbb{R}^{\ell}$ is in $\mathcal{F}$ for any continuous function $g:\mathbb{R}^{p}\to\mathbb{R}^{\ell}$.' This means $\mathcal{F}$ must be closed under post-composition with any continuous function mapping to $\mathbb{R}^{\ell}$. However, the theorem claims this holds 'for any $\ell$', which would require $\mathcal{F}$ to be function-closed for every $\ell$ simultaneously — a much stronger condition. The phrasing is ambiguous: either the theorem is meant to hold for a fixed $\ell$ (and $\mathcal{F}$ is assumed function-closed for that $\ell$), or the paper intends to assert that $\mathcal{F}$ is function-closed for all $\ell$. Clarify which interpretation is intended, and if the latter, verify that the GNN classes satisfy this stronger condition.

comments.title: Unjustified equality for graph-level separation
comments.quote: and when extended with a readout layer, their closures consist of functions $f:\underline{\mathcal{G}_{0}}\to\mathbb{R}^{\ell}$ satisfying $\rho_{0}(\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)})=\rho_{0}(\mathsf{vwl}_{1}^{(t)})\subseteq\rho_{0}(f)$.
comments.explanation: The equality $\rho_0(\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)}) = \rho_0(\mathsf{vwl}_1^{(t)})$ is asserted without justification. The paper's Theorem 4.3 gives $\rho_1(\mathsf{cr}^{(t)}) = \rho_1(\mathsf{GTL}^{(t)}(\Omega))$ for vertex-level separation, but no theorem or lemma establishes the claimed equality for graph-level separation. The relationship between guarded color refinement (gcr) and vertex-WL (vwl) at the graph level is not derived or referenced. The authors should either provide a proof or reference for this equality, or remove it if it is not needed for the argument.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about (k+1)-IGN closure
comments.quote: Furthermore, Theorem 5.1 implies that $\overline{(k+1)\text{-}\mathsf{IGN}_{\ell}}$ consists of functions $f$ satisfying $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{k}^{(\infty)})\subseteq\rho_{1}(f)$ and $\rho_{0}(\mathsf{gwl}_{k}^{(\infty)})\subseteq\rho_{0}(f)$, a case left open in *Azizian and Lelarge (2021)*.
comments.explanation: The claim that Theorem 5.1 implies this characterization is not fully justified. Theorem 5.1 states that $(k+1)\text{-}\mathsf{IGN}_{\ell}$ is a $k$-MPNN, but the approximation result (Corollary 6.2) requires that the class be concatenation-closed and function-closed. The paper does not verify that $(k+1)\text{-}\mathsf{IGN}_{\ell}$ satisfies these closure properties. Moreover, the claim that this case was 'left open' in Azizian and Lelarge (2021) is not supported by a citation or explanation of what exactly was open. The authors should either provide the missing verification or clarify the assumptions under which the claim holds.

comments.title: Missing verification of closure for eGINs and k-FGNNs
comments.quote: Similarly, $\mathsf{eGIN}_{\ell}^{(t)}$s are $1$-$\mathsf{MPNNs}^{(t)}$, so $\overline{\mathsf{eGIN}_{\ell}^{(t)}}$ contains any function satisfying $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{wl}_{1}^{(t)})\subseteq\rho_{1}(f)$; and when extended with a readout layer, their closures consist of functions $f:\underline{\mathcal{G}_{0}}\to\mathbb{R}^{\ell}$ satisfying $\rho_{0}(\mathsf{gcr}^{(t)})=\rho_{0}(\mathsf{vwl}_{1}^{(t)})\subseteq\rho_{0}(f)$. Finally, $k$-$\mathsf{FGNN}_{\ell}^{(t)}$s are $k$-$\mathsf{MPNNs}^{(t)}$, so $\overline{k\text{-}\mathsf{FGNN}_{\ell}^{(t)}}$ consist of functions $f$ such that $\rho_{1}(\mathsf{vwl}_{k}^{(t)})\subseteq\rho_{1}(f)$.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts eGINs and k-FGNNs are 1-MPNNs and k-MPNNs respectively, but does not verify that these classes satisfy the concatenation-closed and MLP-closed properties required by Corollary 6.2. Without this verification, the claimed characterizations of their closures are not justified. The authors should either prove that eGINs and k-FGNNs satisfy these closure properties or state the additional assumptions needed.

comments.title: Missing definition of graph size n
comments.quote: We assume that graphs have size $n$, so $V_{G}$ consists of $n$ vertices and we often identify $V_{G}$ with $[n]$.
comments.explanation: The paper states 'We assume that graphs have size $n$' but does not specify whether $n$ is fixed or varies across the domain. If $n$ is fixed, then $\mathcal{G}$ is the set of all graphs on exactly $n$ vertices, which is a finite set. This is a nontrivial restriction because many GNN approximation results consider graphs of arbitrary size. The paper should clarify whether $n$ is fixed or whether the results extend to graphs of varying sizes. Add a sentence after the quoted text: 'Unless otherwise stated, $n$ is a fixed positive integer; extensions to varying $n$ are discussed in Section X.'

comments.title: Inconsistent use of permutation variable in invariance definition
comments.quote: A function $f:\mathcal{G}_{0}\to\mathbb{F}$ is called *invariant* if $f(G)=f(\sigma\star G)$ for any permutation $\pi$.
comments.explanation: The quantifier says 'for any permutation $\pi$' but the expression uses $\sigma\star G$. The variable $\pi$ is not defined; the intended variable is $\sigma$. This is a minor inconsistency that could confuse readers. The sentence should read 'for any permutation $\sigma$' to match the notation used in the expression.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'augmented GNNs' and treewidth bound
comments.quote: We also consider “augmented” $\mathsf{GNN}$s, which are combined with a preprocessing step in which higher-order graph information is computed. In the supplementary material (Section D.3) we show how $\mathsf{TL}$ encodes the preprocessing step, and how this leads to separation bounds in terms of $k$-$\mathsf{WL}$, where $k$ depends on the treewidth of the graph information used.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'treewidth of the graph information' is ambiguous. The paper earlier defines treewidth for TL expressions (via the treewidth of the associated hypergraph), but here it refers to the treewidth of the 'graph information' itself, which is unclear. It is not specified whether this refers to the treewidth of the precomputed graph features (e.g., a subgraph) or the treewidth of the TL expression encoding the preprocessing. The authors should clarify this definition and explain how the treewidth of the preprocessing step translates into a bound on the number of indices in TL.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a theoretical framework using tensor languages to analyze the separation power of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in terms of Weisfeiler-Leman (WL) tests. The authors introduce a specification language for GNNs and derive bounds on their expressiveness based on the number of indices and summation depth of tensor expressions. The work recovers and extends existing results for architectures like GINs and k-MPNNs, and addresses an open problem regarding the separation power of (k+1)-IGNs.

The paper provides a valuable theoretical contribution by offering a unified framework to analyze GNN expressiveness. The reduction of GNN analysis to tensor language properties is elegant and could simplify the design of expressive architectures. However, the contribution is primarily theoretical, with limited discussion of practical implications or empirical validation of the proposed bounds.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Empirical Validation of Theoretical Bounds: The paper derives theoretical bounds on the separation power of various GNN architectures (e.g., GINs, k-MPNNs) using the tensor language framework. However, it lacks empirical evidence to validate these theoretical claims. For instance, while the paper states that GINs are bounded by t rounds of color refinement, it does not provide experimental results showing that GINs fail to separate graphs that are not separable by t rounds of CR. Similarly, the claims about the separation power of (k+1)-IGNs being bounded by k-WL are not empirically demonstrated. Without such validation, readers cannot assess the practical relevance of these theoretical distinctions. The paper should include experiments that test the theoretical predictions on standard graph benchmarks, such as distinguishing non-isomorphic graphs that are known to be separable by specific WL rounds but not others. This would strengthen the paper's claims and provide a more concrete understanding of the GNN limitations discussed.

- Missing Discussion on Computational Complexity of k-MPNNs: The paper introduces k-MPNNs as a natural extension of MPNNs based on tensor language expressions with k+1 indices. However, it does not discuss the computational complexity of implementing these higher-order GNNs. While the theoretical framework provides insights into expressiveness, the practical feasibility of training or even evaluating k-MPNNs on large-scale graphs is not addressed. Higher-order aggregations (e.g., using k+1 indices) can lead to significant computational overhead, potentially negating the benefits of increased expressiveness. The paper should discuss the trade-off between expressiveness and computational efficiency, perhaps by analyzing the time complexity of evaluating k-MPNNs or discussing approximation techniques to mitigate this cost. This is a critical gap for researchers interested in applying these theoretical insights to real-world problems.

- Ambiguity in the Definition of Separation Power: The paper defines separation power using equivalence relations $ho_1(	au)$ and $ho_0(	au)$ based on the ability of functions to distinguish vertices or graphs. However, the definition is somewhat abstract and does not clearly connect to the practical goal of graph classification or node prediction tasks. For example, a function might be able to separate two vertices in a theoretical sense but fail to do so in a way that is useful for a downstream task like node classification. The paper should clarify how the theoretical notion of separation power relates to the performance of GNNs on standard machine learning tasks. It would be beneficial to discuss whether maximizing separation power (in the theoretical sense) necessarily leads to better performance on practical tasks, or if there are other factors (e.g., overfitting, data quality) that are more important. This clarification would help readers understand the practical implications of the theoretical results.

- Incomplete Treatment of Non-Linearity and Activation Functions: The paper focuses on linear algebraic computations and summation aggregations in its tensor language framework. While it mentions that activation functions and MLPs can be included in the function set $	au$, it does not analyze how non-linearity affects the separation power of GNNs. In standard GNN theory, non-linearities are crucial for increasing expressiveness beyond linear transformations. The paper should investigate whether the inclusion of non-linear functions in $	au$ changes the separation bounds derived in Section 4. Specifically, does the presence of non-linearities allow GNNs to exceed the separation power predicted by the number of indices and summation depth? If not, the paper should explain why. This is an important question because many modern GNN architectures rely heavily on non-linearities to achieve their expressiveness.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add empirical experiments that validate the theoretical separation bounds for key GNN architectures (e.g., GINs, k-MPNNs) on standard graph benchmarks.

- Discuss the computational complexity of implementing higher-order GNNs (k-MPNNs) and the trade-off between expressiveness and efficiency.

- Clarify the connection between the theoretical notion of separation power and practical performance on graph learning tasks.

- Analyze the impact of non-linear activation functions on the separation power of GNNs within the tensor language framework.

comments.title: Indexing Discrepancy in Abstract vs Section 5
comments.quote: 19a)* by showing that the separation power of Invariant Graph Networks ($k$-IGNs), introduced by *Maron
comments.explanation: The abstract claims $k$-IGNs are bounded by $(k-1)$-WL, while Section 5 explicitly analyzes $(k+1)$-IGNs and shows they are bounded by $k$-WL. This discrepancy is confusing and potentially incorrect. Please verify the indexing in the abstract and ensure consistency with the detailed results in Section 5.

comments.title: Misleading Equivariance/Invariance Phrasing
comments.quote:
.

Any function $f:\mathcal{G}_{s}\to\mathbb{R}^{\ell}$ represented in $\mathsf{TL}(\Omega)$ is equivariant (invariant if $s=0$).
comments.explanation: The proposition states that functions on $\mathcal{G}_0$ (graphs) are equivariant, noting they are invariant if $s=0$. This phrasing is misleading because equivariance is a property of functions on $\mathcal{G}_s$ with $s>0$ (vertex embeddings), while invariance is the property of functions on $\mathcal{G}_0$ (graph embeddings). The parenthetical note suggests equivariance is the primary property, which contradicts standard terminology. Please clarify the distinction between equivariant and invariant functions in the context of $\mathcal{G}_s$.

comments.title: Overly Broad Claim on Layer-Round Correspondence
comments.quote: This will allow for measuring the separation power of classes of GNNs in terms of their number of layers.
comments.explanation: The claim that the number of layers directly corresponds to the number of WL rounds is too strong without qualification. A single GNN layer can implement multiple rounds of WL if it aggregates information from distant neighbors. The text should clarify that this correspondence holds for specific architectures (like GINs) or under restricted aggregation forms, rather than making a blanket statement about all GNNs.

comments.title: Counter-Intuitive Claim on Non-Linearity
comments.quote: All separation results for $\mathsf{TL}(\Omega)$ and fragments thereof hold irregardless of the chosen functions in $\Omega$, including when no functions are present at all. Function applications hence do not add expressive power.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that non-linear functions do not increase the separation power of tensor language expressions. This is a counter-intuitive claim that requires careful justification. While the paper notes that summation and multiplication are sufficient, the reader will wonder why non-linearities are irrelevant to the *separation* power (as opposed to approximation power). Please explain why non-linearities do not help distinguish graphs that are indistinguishable by linear combinations of neighbor features.

comments.title: Placeholder Ethics Statement
comments.quote: The results in this paper do not include misleading claims; their correctness is theoretically verified. Related work is accurately represented.
comments.explanation: This statement is a placeholder that fails to address the ethical implications of the research. A proper ethics statement should discuss potential biases in datasets, the societal impact of theoretical findings, or limitations in the study's scope. Please provide a substantive discussion relevant to the paper's content.
